data.title: Gold Technical: Bullish Acceleration In Progress Reinforced By 'Trump Trade'
data.description:
This is a follow-up analysis of our prior report “Gold Technical: Poised for a potential bullish breakout as US CPI looms” published on 11 September 2024.

Since our last publication, the price actions of Gold (XAU/USD) have staged a bullish breakout and cleared above the US$2,640/715 medium-term resistance. The yellow metal has rallied by 9.6% from 11 September to Monday, 21 October current all-time high of US$2,740.

“Trump Trade” narrative has reinforced the uptrend in Gold

The “Trump Trade” narrative has gained traction in the recent week due to rising odds of Trump winning the US election based on data from betting markets (60%Trump versus 39% Harris based on Real Clear Politics data as of 20 October).

Given that Trump’s “generous” corporate tax cuts proposal to reduce the tax rate to 15% from 21% will likely widen the US federal deficit further, in turn leading the market to question the credit standing of the US government (such as the prospect of more frequent government shutdowns) that may see an erosion of confidence in US Treasuries and strengthened Gold (XAU/USD).

Gold is being used as tail-risk hedge

Trump’s proposed tax and trade tariffs policies are likely to reignite upward inflationary pressures in the medium to long-term.

In addition, geopolitical risk premium has not been totally eradicated yet in the Middle East due to the ongoing Israel-Hamas war.

Hence, higher inflationary pressure and an increase in geopolitical risk premium are deadly concoctions that may lead to stagflation which in turn can spark a potential risk-off episode in the global financial markets.

In the lens of technical analysis, the ratio chart of S&P 500 over Gold (XAU/USD) together with its monthly RSI momentum indictor of the S&P 500 / Gold (XAU/USD) ratio has displayed a significant underperformance of S&P 500 against Gold (XAU/USD) since February 2024 (see Fig 1).

Similar observation has been detected in the past during the peak of the Dot.com bubble in August 2000 before the S&P 500 staged a major correction of 35% over the next two years.

Therefore, the recent heightened demand for Gold (XAU/USD) is likely reinforced by portfolio tail-risk hedging activities as well.

Medium-term uptrend remains intact

The price actions of Gold (XAU/USD) have been trading firmly above its rising 20-day and 50-day moving averages since 9 August 2024 supported by a parallel ascending trendline support seen in the daily RSI momentum indicator (see Fig 2).

These observations suggest that medium-term upside momentum remains intact for Gold (XAU/USD).

Watch the US$2,590 key medium-term pivotal support for the potential continuation of the impulsive up move sequence for the next medium-term resistances to come in at US$2,850/886 and US$2,933 (also the upper boundary of the medium-term ascending channel from 15 February 2024 low).

On the flipside, failure to hold at US$2,590 negates the bullish tone for a multi-week correction sequence to unfold within its major uptrend phase to expose the next medium-term supports at US$2,484 and US$2,360 (also the 200-day moving average).

Original Post

data.title: Tencent: A Potential Bold Move To Spark Gaming Revival?
data.description:
Bloomberg reported that Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) (OTCPK:TCTZF) and Ubisoft’s founding Guillemot family are considering a potential buyout of the video game developer (LINK). Although details of the potential buyout have yet to be disclosed, we believe that it makes strategic sense for Tencent to consider increasing its stake in the video game developer given the structural challenges of diminishing innovative DNA and the lack of global footprint that the company has been facing over the past five years.

Despite the stock being a consensus buy amongst both China-focused and global investor bases, given its blue-chip status, we are bearish on Tencent due to the following reasons:

First, the gaming division is facing innovation challenges, as the company has been unable to create a blockbuster IP that is competitive on a global scale.

Second, growth in advertising will likely be handicapped by the plateauing of the ecosystem user base, which is driven predominantly by WeChat. Although e-commerce advertising and streaming videos have been doing relatively well, it is worth noting that the combined revenue contribution to the Tencent revenue profile is estimated to be less than 5% and unlikely to move the needle.

Finally, fintech’s growth is essentially over, given the already high penetration of digital payment and wealth management products amongst the Chinese population and the uncertain regulatory environment involving fintech. As such, this business segment is unlikely to fetch a premium valuation that investors had expected five years ago.

With Tencent trading at 20x forward consensus 2024E P/E revenue growing at +8% and earnings increasing at +37%, we feel that the valuation is unjustified and could be perceived as inflated, given its status as a crowded trade. Investors are better off with PDD (PDD) because it benefits from China’s macro environment and global growth potential (LINK).

As for the potential buyout of Ubisoft, there is a higher probability that this transaction will close, as it makes strategic sense for both Tencent and the Guillemot family.

For Tencent, the rationale is clearly to capture higher ownership of a proven video game developer, leverage its ownership to publish AAA titles in China and develop similar IP catered to the more mainstream Chinese gamer base. This is mainly an essential strategy given Tencent’s unproven ability to create original game IP that can capture the mindshare of the global audience.

Notably, League of Legends was developed by US-based Riot Games, which Tencent acquired in 2011. This followed another major, blockbuster acquisition of Supercell in 2016. Since then, Tencent’s “blockbuster” titles, which comprised most of its gaming revenue, consisted of Honor of Kings, a mobile copycat of League of Legends, and Peacekeeper Elite, a copycat of PUBG, which Korea-based Krafton developed.

Ubisoft’s proven track record in open-world AAA titles, such as Assassin’s Creed and the first-person-shooter Rainbow Six franchise, is critical for Tencent to gradually monetize these assets in China for the Chinese audience. We note that the Assassin’s Creed series has never had a China-focused story, with most of the series based on The Third Crusade, the Italian Renaissance, the French Revolution, or the Viking’s Invasion of England, to name a few. The latest Assassin’s Creed Shadows is focused on Japan’s Sengoku period, and we suspect that Tencent could be looking to leverage Ubisoft’s expertise in Assassin’s Creed to create an open-world game based on part of Chinese history, most likely the Romance of The Three Kingdoms period. Creating an open-world AAA title based on Chinese history has been a proven success, judging by the reception of the Black Myth Wukong, which has hit close to $1 billion in sales since launching in August (LINK).

On the other hand, the rationale for the Guillemot family could be purely financial. Given the disappointing performance of Ubisoft shares, down 50% before the rally on this news and with earnings downgrade from poor performance, the Guillemot family may be looking for a strategic investor for cooperation. Although we do not expect the Guillemot family to sell Ubisoft to Tencent outright, given their historical preference of keeping this asset and that such a transaction would not address Tencent’s fundamental weakness of creating hits via M&A, we do expect Tencent to play a more significant role if this buyout would occur. After all, China remains the world’s largest gaming market and the most extensive gamer base. A strategically crafted partnership between the two parties could result in a higher valuation for Ubisoft.

In conclusion, although we are bearish on Tencent’s long-term fundamentals, we are optimistic about this potential buyout. We believe this would be highly accretive to Tencent’s gaming business and could drive revenue reacceleration in the medium to long term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: We're Not Enthusiastic About Snap
data.description:
Snapchat (NYSE:SNAP) is one of the best known social media companies, with a market capitalization of almost $18 billion. The company does have a much lower valuation than many peer companies, such as Meta, as the company struggles to both maintain growth and revenue. As we'll see throughout this article, the company's focus outside of the U.S., will hurt future returns.

Snap 2Q 2024 Financial Results

In the company's most recent quarter, it saw more than $1.2 billion of revenue at $2.86 per user.

The company has lower revenue per user than many of its peers, highlighting continuing competition. The company has a negative margin and saw $250 million in net loss. The company's FCF remains negative as well with the company's TTM FCF at just a hair above $0 at $15 million. The company maintains $3.1 billion in cash and marketable securities.

With a $17 billion in market cap, and a company that's been around for more than a decade, and public for a while, the company needs to generate FCF to come close to its valuation at some point. So far, the company has shown an inability to do just that.

Snap Segment Performance

Snapchat has seen some growth across its markets, however, its most profitable markets, such as North America and Europe have stagnated.

The company saw 9% global growth, but in North America it remained completely flat. Europe only saw 3% growth. Given that Europe + North America make up more than 80% of the company's revenue stagnation in these markets isn't made up by growth in the Rest of the World that the company is seeing.

Relatively modest performance in users, combined with weak financials, show Snap's current struggles.

Snap Costs and Growth

Snapchat has seen its growth margin taper downwards as costs have remained lofty.

The company's adjusted gross margin is down to 53% as cost of revenue has gone up from 44% to 47% of revenue. The company's primary cost is infrastructure costs which were ~$350 million for the quarter. The company's content & developer partner costs have remained roughly flat, however, the company's advertising costs have gone up.

Snapchat also has a hidden employee cost in the company's continued dilution through stock-based awards. The company's stock-based awards add roughly 40 million shares on an annual basis which comes out to $100 million per quarter in hidden dilution that the company isn't accounting for in its expenses.

That's disappointing for a company with declining margin that's already losing money on a per-share basis. The company seems to be losing popularity, and it should have a P/E ~10 with its stagnating financials to justify a potential investment despite declines. Being at negative profits in such a position shows the company as a poor investment.

Our View

Snapchat has seen consistent struggles as usage continues to go down.

The company has seen substantial stagnation especially among ages 19-24, at the top end, as these ages are much less interested in growing Snapchat usage. Among older people (25+) the company is seeing a substantial decline. Across the board usage is declining, which explains the company's weak ARPU, which will hurt future returns.

Facebook was the social network that changed the trend of social networks occasionally dying out, as seen with the popularity of MySpace at one point. Snapchat, unfortunately, doesn't seem to be avoiding that trend, and seems to be dying a slow death. That fundamentally, along with poor financials at its relative peak, makes the company a poor investment.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that Snap does have hundreds of millions of regular users and the company is growing its revenue from these users. The company could manage to grow enough to justify its valuation, especially if it finds a way to become popular. That could enable increased shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Snap trades at an incredibly lofty valuation given the company's negative earnings. The company has a market capitalization of ~$17 billion, and the company's negative earnings, along with continued dilution of $100 million / quarter should minimize any ability to continue driving future shareholder returns.

Additionally, the company is seeing usage go down. We see the company as not an exception to the regular decline of social networks, as what was once seen with MySpace. We expect the company to fade into obscurity making it a poor long-term investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Why Lam Research Is At A Make-Or-Break Level Before Earnings
data.description:
Lam Research Corporation (NASDAQ:LRCX) is about to report earnings on October 23 after the closing bell, and this may be a make-or-break moment for the stock. Why do I say this? Because the stock is at a critical support level, technically speaking, this earnings report could be the catalyst to make it bounce off that level or completely break down.

In this article, I'll analyze LRCX's fundamentals and valuation going into the earnings report, and then I'll talk about the chart.

My thoughts on LRCX are as follows: LRCX is an excellent growth stock. It's a key player in a very important industry (semiconductor equipment manufacturing), and its profitability metrics clearly highlight its competitive advantage. Its valuation is a bit high if you look at its past multiples, but the stock can still see decent returns from here in the next couple of years.

However, the question is whether you should buy it before earnings or not. Personally, I'm staying out of the stock for now. Since its valuation is a bit higher than usual, even with the stock down 35% from its all-time high, I wouldn't feel confident holding it into earnings. This is especially true considering the potential for the support level breaking if the earnings report goes bad. Thus, I rate LRCX as a Hold.

What Makes Lam Research So Important?

I don't want to explain this in too much detail, as I assume that most people reading this are familiar with what the company does, but I'll explain briefly. Lam Research is an important player in the semiconductor industry because it provides the equipment for etching, deposition, and cleaning in the chip-making process. Its customers include some big dogs like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) and Intel Corporation (INTC), who rely on the company's equipment to make chips.

Why Lam Research Is A High-Quality Company

Lam Research has many traits of a high-quality company. One of the first things I check for when analyzing a company is if it generates consistent and growing cash flows and if its profit margins are stable or growing. I also check for high returns on capital and long-term revenue growth. Lam Research fits the criteria.

First, its 10-year revenue CAGR is 12.46%. Further, we can see that its cash flows have quickly grown over the years. Free cash flow is high also because the firm's CapEx is relatively low. That's because it doesn't have to spend money on building fabs like Taiwan Semiconductor does. It just creates the equipment that's used as part of the chip-making process, which is a lot less capital-intensive.

Now, take a look at its gross profit margin, which has increased over the years, signaling a competitive advantage, as competitors haven't been able to eat away at profits.

The company also has a consistently high cash return on invested capital [CROIC], which is another sign of a competitive advantage. Again, this can partly be attributed to the capital-light nature of the company. Its TTM CROIC comes in at 31.2%, while its five-year average is 27.8%. Compare this to capital-intensive companies like TSM, whose TTM CROIC is 17.9%, or Intel's (-7.7%, per FinBox), and you'll see why I like LRCX.

The Valuation Is Historically High But Still Reasonable

Looking at the chart of LRCX's 10-year normalized P/E ratio, you'll see that it has come down recently but is still relatively high. 25.3x may not seem too high for a company that's expected to see 18.15% EPS growth next year and 23% growth in 2026, but what you need to realize is that LRCX is a cyclical company. I assume that's why LRCX and many other chip companies tend to have lower multiples (at least historically). Perhaps if the buzz around AI dies down eventually, LRCX stock will return to a more reasonable multiple of around 20x or even 18x (roughly eyeballing the average multiple in the chart).

If it does, that will hamper its total return potential, but it doesn't mean that the overall returns will be bad, depending on how low the multiple gets.

I'll present three scenarios for total returns. For all three, let's assume that LRCX meets the June 2026 consensus EPS estimate of $4.40 per share. And I don't think this is unreasonable to expect. That's because, in the past two years, analyst estimates for LRCX have been relatively stable, so maybe that trend can continue. Also, to keep it simple, let's assume that LRCX maintains a steady quarterly dividend of $0.23 per share.

Scenario 1: LRCX will have a 20x multiple by the time the June 2026 annual results are out. This gives you a share price of $88 ($4.40 x 20), and you'll also receive $1.61 per share in dividend payments, as seven quarters will have passed. Adding the two gives you a CAGR of 12.57% over that time frame. Not bad.

Scenario 2: LRCX has a 19x by then. Including the dividends, this gives you a CAGR of 9.4%. That's okay but not special.

Scenario 3: LRCX has an 18x by then. This gives you a 6.1% CAGR. Not acceptable.

Of course, the multiple can drop even lower, or it can stay elevated if AI hype is maintained. That's hard to predict two years out. But presently, my analysis tells me that LRCX can potentially provide decent returns even if the valuation drops a bit, but there isn't enough of a margin of safety to make me want to buy. When it comes to chip stocks, I'm much more confident in ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) stock, to which I recently gave a Strong Buy rating.

I'm Expecting An EPS Beat

Based on LRCX's earnings track record, I fully expect it to beat earnings forecasts for the upcoming report. Besides a tiny 0.05% miss in the June 2021 quarter, Lam Research has beaten estimates every time since the December 2020 quarter.

Revenue also generally beats expectations, although there are a few more misses in there.

However, just because LRCX is likely to beat estimates doesn't mean that the stock will rise after earnings. Last quarter, it beat both revenue and EPS estimates but still fell by about 10% the following day. Now, let's look at the chart.

The Chart Points To A Make-Or-Break Moment Going Into Earnings

This earnings report will be critical for LRCX because the stock is at a key level. In the chart below, I've highlighted two important levels. First, there's a resistance level at around $86, which used to be a support level. Second, there's a support level near $73, right where the stock is currently. This support level is used to act as resistance, as shown with the arrows. With seven arrows pointing to this spot since the beginning of 2022, it's obviously an important level to watch.

If LRCX breaks lower after earnings, the closest and strongest support would be near $57 (the October 2023 low), or about 22% lower than the last price. This would also bring its valuation closer to its historical levels, so it's not an entirely unlikely scenario.

Nonetheless, LRCX stock fell recently sympathizing with ASML, which fell after its earnings report last week. This could mean that a lot of the downside has been priced in already.

If LRCX rises after earnings on impressive results and increased guidance, I won't feel like I missed out. That's okay. There are plenty of opportunities out there. If it gaps up, I'd potentially wait for a pullback (gap fill) and then buy, depending on how impressed I am with the results. If it gaps down considerably closer to the $57 level (assuming the results are not horrible), I'll look to buy.

The Bottom Line On LRCX Stock

Lam Research is a high-quality company in an important industry, and it has the potential to generate reasonable returns going forward based on its expected EPS growth. It has high returns on invested capital due to its competitive positioning and its capital-light nature. Furthermore, its cash flows and revenues have grown significantly over the years, and its gross profit margin has trended higher as well.

However, the margin of safety is not enough for me to give it a Buy rating, especially since the stock is at a key level going into an earnings report. Even though Lam Research is likely to beat estimates based on its track record, that doesn't mean the stock can't fall. That's what happened to it last quarter.

When looking at its historical valuation, LRCX stock should probably trade at a lower earnings multiple closer to 18-20x due to the cyclical nature of the semiconductor industry. Since its P/E ratio is closer to 25x presently, that can stop the company from generating exceptional returns.

Based on the points mentioned above, I believe LRCX stock is a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold Miners: Likely To Continue Higher
data.description:
The last time we discussed gold in a public article, the yellow metal pulled back from an all-time high "ATH" and was in the $2,300-2,400 range. After several months of constructive price action, gold is in the $2,700-2,800 range and could reach $3,000 by year-end or early 2025. Gold has done very well recently, and for good reason. The Fed is easing rates, which could lead to a significant liquidity influx as lending and the money supply expand in future years.

Gold's price had a lost decade, and it could continue moving higher despite being up by approximately 35% over the last year. Another fascinating factor is that many high-quality gold miners have underperformed the base metal despite the constructive backdrop. Therefore, as gold continues advancing, we could see outperformance from the high-quality gold miners, making them excellent "buy the dips" opportunities.

A Clear Underperformance From The Gold Miners

While gold has gained about 35% over the last 12 months, many miners have done about the same or worse. For instance, Barrick Gold (GOLD) has climbed by around 24% over the last year, while Alamos Gold (AGI) appreciated by around 42%. Gold Fields (GFI) gained about 28% over the previous 12 months. Several bigger miners performed slightly better, as Newmont (NEM) gained about 50%, and Agnico Eagle (AEM) appreciated by around 70% over the previous 52 weeks. Despite some solid performances from the gold sector, we're not witnessing extreme upside price action, and there is likely an explanation for this dynamic.

Why The Underperformance Occurs

Gold miners could have appreciated much more by now. In previous gold bull market cycles, we've witnessed gold miners' outperformance of about 2-3 to 1 vs. gold. However, we're seeing relatively little outperformance from the gold mining space, and this could be because the market doesn't believe that higher gold prices are sustainable. This may be due to the belief that the Fed could "mop up" the excess liquidity when the easing cycle is complete. However, we've witnessed the Fed attempt to unload its balance sheet (unsuccessfully) in the 2014-2018 time frame and during the recent tightening cycle.

The Fed's Balance Sheet (in millions)

The Fed has been able to decrease its balance sheet by around $2 trillion, which is not nothing. However, the Fed has flipped from tightening to easing, and its QT program will probably end soon. Therefore, we could see a repeat of recent cycles, where the Fed goes back to easing, introduces QE, and its balance sheet explodes to new highs. This monetary expansion dynamic is bullish for gold, and we will likely see prices continuing to expand during the monetary easing process.

Gold Miner Earnings Will Likely Be Much Higher

Part of the reason why gold mining stocks have struggled is because many of the earnings estimates appear predicated on lower or declining gold prices. However, the market may be behind the curve because it still hopes the Fed will unwind its enormous balance sheet, and gold prices could decline to $2K or lower. Yet, the reality is that gold could move to $3K or higher, and gold mining profits could skyrocket. Here are several earnings estimates as examples:

Newmont Mining - EPS Estimates

According to consensus projections, Newmont is the most significant gold miner globally, and its forward P/E ratio is below 15. However, Newmont has surpassed analysts' estimates in recent quarters. Also, its EPS estimates continue rising, and NEM could earn substantially more than $4 in EPS next year and over $5 in 2026. NEM trades at around $55. Thus, its forward P/E ratio, relative to 2026 EPS estimates, could be around ten, which is relatively low for a company in NEM's advantage position.

We also see future projections sloping lower, illustrating how many analysts likely factor in a relatively low price for gold in the coming years. On the contrary, demand for gold could remain robust, and gold prices could be substantially higher in 2025 and 2026.

AEM - EPS Estimates

AEM is in a similar spot as near-term EPS estimates continue to rise due to the higher gold price. For perspective, the consensus 2025 EPS estimate was only around $2 during the summer but has recently surged to $4.57.

Oddly, AEM's 2025 EPS projections now eclipse all the 2026-2030 EPS projections. Are analysts attempting to call a top in gold? This appears very peculiar, and AEM's profitability may continue increasing instead of falling steadily after 2025, as the estimates suggest.

When we look at most gold miners' projections, not just AEM, we see a similar underwhelming intermediate and longer-term earnings dynamic. Also, AEM's forward P/E ratio is relatively low, at around 18-19. This dynamic implies that AEM could continue moving higher as further out EPS estimates increase in future quarters.

GOLD - EPS Estimates

Barrick Gold, another top gold miner globally, appears very cheap here relative to its estimates. Barrick has also seen substantially higher than expected income due to the constructive developments in the gold market. However, many analysts factor in peak earnings around 2025 and 2026, then sloping lower, which may not be accurate. On the contrary, Barrick could continue surpassing consensus estimates as it advances.

Reality Vs. Estimates

Barrick's TTM consensus EPS estimate was only $0.84 when, in actuality, Barrick earned $1.02, which is more than a 20% outperformance rate. Next year, the consensus EPS estimate is around $1.82, but a similar outperformance rate would bring the EPS up to about $2.20, implying that Barrick may be dirt cheap now, below 10 times forward earnings estimates.

The Bottom Line

Gold remains in the Goldilocks zone as the Fed eases and the monetary supply expands. Moreover, the easing cycle has just started, and it could last a long time. We may also see elects of QE and other stimuli that will likely benefit gold prices in future years.

Gold also lost a decade as the market was convinced that the Fed could unwind its balance sheet. However, we now see the Fed can only reduce its balance sheet slightly (20% or so) before introducing easing monetary policy again.

Therefore, the market likely sees through this dynamic and could take gold higher for longer before this bull market concludes. Of course, we could have transitory pullbacks and corrections, but Gold's uptrend appears very solid, and gold could move to $3,000 in the next several (3-6 months).

Furthermore, little or no evidence suggests that gold prices will decline in the next few years, making high-quality gold miners optimal investments to capitalize on this massive gold rally. While it should do well, gold miners like NEM, AEM, GOLD, and others could do even better, making these and other gold-related stocks buy on pullbacks as the market corrects.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Otis: A Company With Appeal, But Not Great Valuation
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I'll be taking a look at Otis International (NYSE:OTIS). This company was actually a business with a tradition since 1852 with a long history of developing safety equipment. So the company's spin-off from United Technologies in 2020 as Otis International was only the company going back to its roots as an independent business.

Otis works in the same segment as KONE (OTCPK:KNYJY) - a Finnish business that I have reviewed in the past and works in pretty much the same product range - escalators and moving/access equipment. But Otis is the world's largest manufacturer of such products, with a focus on the aforementioned moving systems including elevators, escalators, and walkways (moving) ones.

What I'm going to do in this article is, offer a review of the business - and see what can be expected from Otis going forward in the next five to 20 years. I have been investing in KONE for about 10 years at this point, but truth be told it has never been one of my most successful plays. I believe part of that is the underlying volatility and correlation to the industrial macro that these companies tend to have. Of course, also the international market and the trends coming from this, in terms of ups and downs as spending for these types of products and systems increases or decreases.

Otis is a BBB rated company, which is good. The dividend is below 1.5%, which is not that good. The company also comes with some interesting fundamentals but has managed to command a fairly steep premium so far.

That's what I'll look at for this business

Otis International - Upside from Moving Systems/Escalators, But At What Price?

Following the spin-off, the share price has mostly moved in the right direction. Investors into Otis shares have averaged an annualized RoR of 14.07% since 2021, or a total RoR of 64.5% inclusive of the company's meager dividend, which is okay overall - and above most market indices. It's a good performance. However, this has also established a fairly steep premiumization based on a double-digit earnings growth on an adjusted basis.

The spin-off was a good choice, I believe, for Otis. Since then, the company has expanded its operating profit (adjusted) by 1.7%, new equipment net share is up, portfolio growth is up, and to date, over 500,000 units are connected to the company's "ONE" IoT platform as of the year-end of the last annual results.

The company's business model is fundamentally attractive, because it operates primarily a service-driven/centric business model, both in net sales, but definitely in operating profit.

This mix and the underlying share price reflect a very solid performance from 2019-2023, with service revenues growing from a net sales level of $7.4B to $8.4B in less than 5 years, as well as a $400M increase in overall operating profit. The company argues that it has multiple growth paths for ensuring top-line growth, including modernization, growing service, and urbanization requiring more volumes of company products. New Equipment shares for the company have grown from 14% in 2012 to upwards of 20% in 2023, and modernization orders have more than doubled since 2021. New Equipment, Modernization, and service make up the company's 40/10/50 sales split that we're looking at.

The company's solid product base is confirmed by its customer retention rate, which at this point for 2024 is above 93%. The churn for this company is very low. The cancellations we saw during COVID-19 and the associated periods (which were significant, no doubt about it), have declined and gone down. Conversion is up, both in terms of the rate, and in terms of an actual number of converted overall units.

The things I'm looking for when it comes to a company like this includes a growing service volume and stable or growing margins - much like with other industrial companies I cover, such as KION (OTCPK:KIGRY) - different segment, but similar trends. Otis has, as of early 2024, delivered a multi-year outlook expecting EPS to grow at 10% at a continued rate. At the same time, the company's fundamentals spell out that the company has zero debt maturing until 2025, it has a growing dividend, it includes inorganic growth in its portfolio targets, and it buys back shares whenever it has excess cash (not that attractive at this valuation, and I hope the company does not do it here).

The company expects to return over $8B in cash to shareholders up to 2028 from today.

The latest results for Otis we have are 2Q24. In 2Q24, we mostly saw exactly what we expected from Otis given its targets. This included service sales growth, organic profit margin expansion of over 100 bps, an EPS growth above that of the 10% expectation, and a modernization order growth of over 13.5%. In short, 2Q24 was an excellent quarter.

It included attractive new orders across the world, where I have no doubt the company "beat out" players like KONE.

The focal points in this quarter should be the improvement in operating profit alongside the EPS growth, in the face of a declining sales number, with total net sales down 3.2%.

Primarily responsible for the decline was a massive decline in Asian new equipment sales, with Asia down 19.6%, most of which was China. I have covered KONE before, and the company's mistake (as I view it), in over-focusing on China. KONE saw very similar decline trends in China over the years, though KONE had an even larger exposure, and as a result, the stock fell quite significantly.

Americas and Europe, as they usually are in this segment and this sector, were relatively stable. But the negative sales development impacted operating profit, with new equipment down and service only up slightly.

Headwinds for the company apart from the decline in sales and China, came from inflation costs, a somewhat less appealing mix, and the overall construction and industrial macro, which continued to impact things here. The company, like most others, has a savings program, which delivers targeted savings of $175M in the run-rate until mid-2025, an estimate the company at this time still holds.

For 2024, the company expects growth of increases of low or mid-single digits in sales for new equipment, with the exception of Asia, which is down to low teens (again, due to China). The "challenges" in New equipment markets in China to which the company alludes are, in my interpretation, related to local competition from lower-cost competitors that have adapted technology, much like with KONE. It's a trend that I recognize. Service numbers for the company are expected to be up 1-3% on an annual basis.

The company slightly lowered its overall FCF outlook, and somewhat mixed its other outlooks (improved some, lowered some) in 2Q24.

I believe it's fair to say that 3Q24, which is arriving soon, will not be a surprise in any way for the market. It's likely to see slight continued growth in KPIs, with impressive YoY growth in EPS. Upsides are, as I expect them, likely to come from volume and productivity, some pricing improvements, input and commodities stabilizing as we've seen over the course of the year, and the company's cost-saving programs. This will, in my estimate, be far better than the impact of Mix and/or the company churn, which is low, as well as the annual wage inflation and other cost inflation, which are declining somewhat.

FX is also a factor to consider, but a small one in my estimate.

Based on this, we can look at the company valuation and what upside we can have for Otis from here.

Otis - Upside Only In The Case Of Premium Valuation, Which I Do Not Want To Apply Here.

Otis currently trades at a $105/share price, which implies a 27.6x normalized P/E ratio. This is high - especially for an industrial manufacturer and player, even with the comparative lack of volatility compared to other industrials, we have to look at here.

The company does have a good earnings forecast accuracy. It never misses its forecast targets, either hits or beats them since the spin-off with a 10-20% margin of error, which is impressive.

The company's average trading level in terms of P/E comes to around 26.4x. The company is therefore, at current valuations, not attractively valued and relies on earnings growth and dividends to deliver alpha to its investors. While we do have earnings growth, the dividends we have are meager. Even at a conservative 9% EPS growth on an adjusted basis, we do not see any more than an 8.5% annualized RoR based on a 26.4x P/E on a forward basis. Even if we were to estimate the company at 29x P/E, which is over 10% of the company's already-premiumized valuation, this would still not mean that the company generated annualized RoR in excess of 13.1% until 2026E based on current numbers. (Source: F.A.S.T Graphs paywalled Link).

Based on these factors and numbers, I view the company's valuation as excessive for what it offers; ergo, it's overvalued. As a consequence, I cannot, in good conscience, rate this company anything but a "Hold".

When it comes to my PT for Otis, I look for the average price at which I can find a conservative upside to a 15% annualized inclusive of the dividend. As of October 2024, this comes to around $85/share, which in turn is about 23x P/E to the 2024E results. I therefore give the company a target of $85/share in order for me to "Buy" the company at this particular point in time.

Sensitivities that could affect this rating have been taken into consideration. Any excessive or less interesting appeals/upsides than the ones we see here, such as lower or greater EPS than expected, still do not change this target. What would change it is an outsized result either positively or negatively in 3Q and 4Q, which is why I'll update for 4Q, and a small edit for 3Q if it changes my thesis in any way. I do not see a reason to use a less or more premiumized multiple for the valuation at this time.

Analysts give Otis a range of $79/share to $120/share, with an average of $101, meaning the company is now 3.9% overvalued according to this average. 14 analysts are at only 4 with a "BUY", the rest at "Hold" and even a few at "underperform". (Source: Seeking Alpha estimates)

My estimate is therefore that this company has reached the heights it can - and I'm on the "bench" with this one.

The thesis for Otis is as follows.

Thesis

  • Otis is a world-leading equipment and industrial manufacturer. It works in areas of elevators, escalators, and various "movement"-enabling technologies that make it a crucial company for the modern world, and one likely to see growth from the current demographics and modernization.
  • The company's spin-off was a successful one in my estimate. We're seeing volume growth, profit growth, and cost improvements which are, in my view, likely to stick. Based on company fundamentals and a good valuation, I'm positively inclined towards Otis, despite its meager sub-1.5% dividend at the time of writing this article.
  • However, I would still want an upside of over 15% annualized on a conservative set of estimates in order to put capital to work at a somewhat decent risk/reward ratio. For that reason, I give the company a "Hold" here, as it does not measure up to this.
  • I give Otis a PT of $85/share to begin with, at which point I could see myself investing in the company.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills three out of three quality indicators, but none of two valuation indicators. Based on this, and a valuation target of $85/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Hold" rating as of October 2024.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Be Cautious About Buying The Dip
data.description:
Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO)(NONOF) is one of the companies and stocks I covered regularly in the past. And the reason I pay close attention to the Danish diabetes company is rather simple – it is one of the biggest positions in my portfolio (and one of the most successful investments in recent years). And while I had been bullish about Novo Nordisk for several years, I have recently become more cautious.

In my last article, which was published at the end of May 2024, I was also rather cautious and called Novo Nordisk not the best investment. Since the article was published, the stock declined about 13.5% and Novo Nordisk is now trading more than 20% below its previous all-time high, and we could argue that Novo Nordisk is in a bear market. And we can also raise the question if we should invest in Novo Nordisk again before the company will report third quarter results at the beginning of November.

In the following article, I will look at several arguments that are speaking for Novo Nordisk as an investment, and we will also discuss the reasons for caution. And we start with the first positive argument for Novo Nordisk – the continuing great performance and high-growth rates.

First-Half Results

And when looking at the results for the first half of fiscal 2024, we see high-growth rates continuing. Net sales increased from DKK 107,667 million in the first half of fiscal 2023 to DKK 133,409 million in the first half of fiscal 2024 – resulting in 23.9% year-over-year top-line growth. Operating profit increased 18.2% year-over-year from DKK 48,895 million in H1/23 to DKK 57,780 million in H1/24. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from DKK 8.71 in the first half of the previous year to DKK 10.17 in the first half of this year – resulting in 16.8% year-over-year growth.

Looking at the different segments, the picture for Novo Nordisk is similar to previous quarters. Total insulin sales also increased 9.2% year-over-year from DKK 24,697 million in H1/23 to DKK 26,977 million in H1/24. In the last few years, insulin sales did not always grow. Nevertheless, the drivers of growth for Novo Nordisk were GLP-1 sales as well as anti-obesity sales.

Total obesity care sales increased from DKK 18,148 million in H1/23 to DKK 24,939 million in H1/24 – resulting in 37.4% year-over-year growth (and Wegovy sales increased 74% year-over-year to DKK 21,036 million). But the most important segment is remaining GLP-1, which increased sales from DKK 54,736 million in H1/23 to DKK 72,017 in H1/24 – resulting in 31.6% year-over-year growth. And here it was especially Ozempic driving growth. Revenue from Ozempic increased from DKK 41,741 million in H1/23 to DKK 56,685 million – resulting in 35.8% year-over-year growth.

What To Expect

And when looking at the guidance for fiscal 2024, Novo Nordisk is expecting growth rates to continue. At constant exchange rates, Novo Nordisk is expecting its top line to grow between 22% and 28%. And while management raised expectations for top-line growth, expectations for operating profit growth were lowered a little bit. But Novo Nordisk is still expecting growth rates between 20% and 28%.

Novo Nordisk is still warning about continued Ozempic shortage that will last until the fourth quarter. These shortages apparently occur especially in the European Union, while in the United States all doses of Ozempic are currently available. Nevertheless, the shortage is still a strong hint of the extremely high demand and Novo Nordisk might be able to grow revenue even at a higher pace if production capacity could be increased.

Novo Nordisk is trying to ramp up production capacity and, as a consequence, capital expenditures will also increase in the years to come. But of course, this might also lead to a lower free cash flow as Novo Nordisk is expecting to spend a low double-digit percentage of sales as CapEx.

But despite the still high growth rates management is expecting for fiscal 2024, we should also not ignore that analysts are lowering estimates a little bit for the years to come. When looking at the earnings revision data, we see estimates for earnings per share being lowered a little bit. But of course, we should be careful not to mix different arguments here: Although analysts are lowered expectations, Novo Nordisk is still expected to grow at an extremely high pace.

Management Planning Ahead

When talking about Novo Nordisk, we are mostly talking about GLP-1 and obesity sales. Nevertheless, we should not ignore that the company has other business segments as well, although these business segments are not really growing and are contributing only a small part to overall revenue.

Much more important is the growth potential Novo Nordisk has due to future products and its pipeline. And very recently it was reported that a study showed how an oral formulation of semaglutide was able to significantly cut the risk of suffering a major cardiovascular event (such as a stroke). According to the Phase III study, called SOUL, patients who were given the oral semaglutide had a 14% lower risk of experiencing a major adverse cardiovascular event.

And that is just one example of how Novo Nordisk is planning ahead and trying to ensure growth in the years to come. In the last few years, Novo Nordisk is focusing more and more on new therapeutic areas, which it calls its emerging therapy areas. These include CVD (cardiovascular diseases), MASH (metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis), CKD (chronic kidney disease) and Alzheimer’s disease. And there are huge overlaps between patients suffering from type 1 or 2 diabetes as well as obesity and these four therapy areas. The following chart demonstrates this by using MASH as an example.

Novo Nordisk also started research on Alzheimer’s disease, a therapeutic area its competitor Eli Lilly (LLY) is already focusing on for a long time. Management is expecting its semaglutide to be used here for treatment as well.

We can also look at the pipeline and must admit that Novo Nordisk has a strong pipeline right now, with several pharmaceuticals from different therapy areas being already in phase III.

Competition

So far, we provided several reasons to be rather bullish and optimistic about Novo Nordisk. And as I am being an investor and are planning to hold the stock for the long run, I am certainly optimistic. Nevertheless, we should not ignore that Eli Lilly is a serious competitor for Novo Nordisk and when looking at the recent numbers, Eli Lilly is outperforming Novo Nordisk.

When comparing Mounjaro to Ozempic and Zepbound to Wegovy, Eli Lilly is reporting much higher growth rates. In H1/24 (we are using these numbers as Novo Nordisk did not report third quarter results yet), Ozempic grew from DKK 41,741 million to DKK 56,685 million (resulting in 35.8% year-over-year growth) while Mounjaro grew from $1,548 million to $4,897 million (resulting in 216% year-over-year growth). And while Wegovy grew from DKK 12,081 million to DKK 21,036 million in H1/24 (resulting in 74.1% year-over-year growth), Zepbound generated $1,761 million in H1/24 compared to $0 in the same timeframe last year (the product was not yet on the market).

Of course, Novo Nordisk has its product already longer on the market, making it more difficult to grow at a high pace and Novo Nordisk is already generating twice the revenue compared to Eli Lilly at this point.

But the launch of Zepbound certainly went well for Eli Lilly and when looking at the number of new weekly prescriptions, we not only see Zepbound starting much better than Wegovy, but we also see Eli Lilly almost catching up to Novo Nordisk.

And when looking at GLP-1 (Ozempic and Mounjaro), we see a similar picture. For Novo Nordisk, we see a lower number of new prescriptions, while the number of new prescriptions for Eli Lilly is stagnating. Just to be clear: This still means both companies are growing sales for these products, as new prescriptions are a strong indicating for additional patients and therefore higher revenue in the coming weeks and months.

There might be a simple and obvious reason for Eli Lilly taking market shares at a high pace and becoming a serious competitor for Ozempic and Wegovy. Different studies indicating, that tirzepatide, the medicine or drug Eli Lilly is using for Mounjaro and Zepbound is better than semaglutide, the medicine Novo Nordisk is using for Ozempic and Wegovy. The studies are not only showing that tirzepatide is lowering the HbA1C by 2% to 2.5%, while semaglutide is lowering it only by 1% to 2%. Studies are also showing that weight loss from semaglutide ranged from 6kg to 10kg, while weight loss with tirzepatide ranged from 7kg to 13kg.

And it is not only one article showing the superiority of tirzepatide, but we can mention more example like the following study from July 2024 or the study from which the chart above was taken

Still Expensive

A second point arguing again an investment in Novo Nordisk is the high valuation multiples the stock is currently trading for. At the time of writing, Novo Nordisk is trading for 40 times earnings, and this is not only close to the highest valuation multiples the stock was trading for in the last ten years, but also clearly above the 10-year average of 28.11. When looking at the price-to-free-cash-flow ratio, the picture is similar. Right now, Novo Nordisk is trading for a valuation multiple of 55 and this is almost twice the 10-year average of 30.82. We could make the argument that Novo Nordisk was trading for an even higher P/FCF ratio (almost 90) in the recent past, but that does not eliminate the argument that 55 times free cash flow is extremely expensive.

Aside from looking at simple valuation metrics, we can also use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for Novo Nordisk, and we will once again see that the stock is not cheap. As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate and are using the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (4,465 million). And as basis for our calculation, we can take the midpoint of management’s free cash flow guidance, which is DKK 64 billion. Similar to previous articles, I am now using a more cautious terminal growth rate (although I am still confident Novo Nordisk could be able to grow at 6% annually for a long time). Nevertheless, I am now calculating with 4% growth till perpetuity (in line with CFI suggestions).

And when calculating with these assumptions, Novo Nordisk must grow 22% annually for the next ten years to be fairly valued right now. We can argue that Novo Nordisk grew its bottom line 27.4% in the last three years, and everybody seems optimistic right now that Novo Nordisk can continue to grow at a high pace. However, in the last ten years, earnings per share grew “only” with a CAGR of 14.82%.

Conclusion

I am very bullish about Novo Nordisk long-term and optimistic about its growth potential. But expecting 22% growth for the next ten years seems very optimistic. And I will still remain cautious about Novo Nordisk right now, and I will still rate the stock as a “Hold”.

I see it as a possibility that the stock price for Novo Nordisk will increase again after this 20% setback and that the rally could continue for Novo Nordisk. But I would still not buy the stock at this point as it is still a bit too expensive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Otis Shares On The Rise As Market Tailwinds Lift New Equipment Prospects
data.description:
Since its spin-off from United Technologies Corporation in April 2020, Otis Worldwide Corporation (NYSE:OTIS) has seen its share price increase by over 160%.

Considering the market tailwinds driven by the Chinese $325 billion stimulus and aggressive interest rate cuts by the FED, the company could see its new equipment sales improve significantly in the quarters.

What is remarkable about this company is the fact that it managed to improve earnings solely through its service segment, despite the decline in new equipment sales.

I believe that these market tailwinds could lead to new equipment sales, leading to an improvement in operating cash flows.

There are some risks that should be considered, including weak liquidity ratios (even though the company can easily handle its interest payments). Recently, there's been a drop in new equipment sales, along with a decrease in operating cash flow, which has resulted in a high price-to-cash flow (P/CF) ratio.

I will cover in more detail the rationale behind my Buy rating below. However, for now, I'd like to start with a brief company overview section.

Company Overview

Otis is a global company that manufactures, installs, and maintains elevators, escalators, and moving walkways.

You'll find their products in residential and commercial buildings, as well as infrastructure projects like airports and shopping malls.

The company makes money by selling new elevator and escalator systems and by offering maintenance services and modernization.

As a matter of fact, they earn more from maintenance and modernization services than selling new equipment.

I've included below a breakdown of revenue per business segment from the latest 10-Q.

They operate globally, so I've included a similar breakdown of revenue per geographical area below.

Ownership-wise, I remain disappointed with the less than 1% ownership of common stock among all 21 executives and directors.

As a side note, I favor companies where management has a decent amount of skin in the game (over 20% ownership of common stock).

The company's 5% owners include BlackRock and Vanguard.

Price Action

As seen below, the share price crossed the $100 mark following a 15% rise since mid-September.

As a side note, there appears to be a resistance level that turned into support at $92, but I don't think it's reliable due to the limited trading data.

The correlation of this stock with the specialty industrial machinery industry, the industrials sector, and the S&P 500 is clear by looking at the 2-year performance chart below.

Fundamentals

Correlations aside, let's understand the drivers behind the increase in the share price.

A quick look at the income statement shows a mid to low single-digit revenue growth since Q2, 2023, except in the last quarter.

The decline in revenue in Q2 2024 was due to an 11.4% decrease in product sales during that quarter. This was driven by a double-digit decline in China, and a mid-teens decline in the Americas caused by the impact of elevated interest rates on new equipment orders.

With the recent announcement of China's stimulus package and the FED's interest rate cuts, there's a strong expectation for a recovery in new product sales.

However, despite the decline in revenue in Q2 2024, the company managed to maintain gross income by controlling production costs and benefiting from a deflationary environment in key markets like China.

A quick look at operating and net income YoY growth shows a positive trend.

This growth can be attributed to the 5.1% increase in service revenue, which has higher margins than the new equipment segment.

Additionally, it seems that the company's Uplift program is yielding positive results, with a 5.7% decline in SG&A.

I am particularly impressed with the 15% increase in EPS; however, I have to mention that the company bought back $300 million worth of shares in Q2 alone ($600 million YTD), which contributed to this improvement.

Moving on to the balance sheet, as of June 30, 2024, Otis had a total debt of $7.182 billion, with cash & equivalents adding up to $1.274 billion.

In regard to the debt structure, the company has $5.526 billion in long-term debt and $1.656 billion in short-term borrowings.

Additionally, they have a $1.5 billion unsecured revolving credit facility, which remains fully available, and a $1.5 billion commercial paper program, with only $320 million used so far.

I have to admit that the liquidity ratios of the company don't look particularly attractive, especially the low cash ratio.

Liquidity Ratio Value
Current Ratio 0.79
Quick Ratio 0.71
Cash Ratio 0.12

The weighted average interest rate for long-term debt is only 2.5%. Therefore, Otis has to pay approximately $43 million per quarter due to interest on its debt, which seems a manageable amount when compared to its cash reserves, or cash from operating activities.

Speaking of operating cash flow, there has been a significant decline in the last two quarters, as shown below.

However, I am not overly concerned about this decline. As already mentioned, new equipment orders have been down by 11.4% YoY. This is key because new equipment generates advance deposits, which improve operating cash flow.

In the service segment, cash is typically collected after the work is completed, unlike new equipment, which tends to generate cash ahead. Therefore, I was not surprised to see the $171 million increase in accounts receivable in the first half of the year.

Furthermore, the timing of payments to suppliers was another contributing factor, with a $162 million reduction in accounts payable in the first half of 2024, compared to an increase during the same period in 2023.

Considering the tailwinds coming from lower interest rates and the stimulus pack in China, new equipment sales are bound to recover. Therefore, I expect an improvement in operating cash flows in the upcoming quarters.

Insider / Activist / Institutional Buying Activity

A quick look at the latest insider transactions shows no relevant activity in the past few months. To be transparent, I remain disappointed with the lack of insider buying activity in the last 2 years.

However, on October 10, there was a major 13G released by JP Morgan, disclosing ownership of 27,076,127 shares in Otis. This represents a 6.7% ownership of the company.

Aside from JP Morgan announcement, there was no major buying/selling activity by any major investment fund in the last month.

Valuation

Considering that Otis only began trading as an independent public company on April 3, 2020, and the increase in the share price since the spin-off from United Technologies Corporation, I am not surprised to see most valuation ratios trading close to their historical maximum.

Therefore, I believe it would be more accurate to compare it with its peers rather than relying on its historical values.

If we use P/S, the company seems to be cheap when compared to some of its industry peers.

As a side note, the chart above is arranged in descending order by P/S.

If we arrange the table by P/CF in descending order, then the company doesn't look that cheap.

However, if market conditions improve due to China's stimulus and the FED cutting interest rates, I expect the P/CF ratio to improve in the upcoming quarters.

Conclusion And Some Risks To Consider

In conclusion, I believe the market tailwinds in China, driven by the $325 billion stimulus, and in the US, with the FED cutting interest rates, could support further growth, particularly in the new equipment segment.

The fundamentals of the company seem to support the 40% share price increase in the past year. The debt seems to be quite high, although the company benefits from a low 2.5% weighted average interest rate, making interest payments quite manageable.

There are some risks that should be considered, including the high valuation ratios, especially P/CF, which seems to be quite high when compared to the industry peers. In my view, if the improvements in the new equipment segment don't occur in the next quarters, despite the market tailwinds, there is a high chance of shareholder sentiment cooling off, potentially leading to a selloff if the company doesn't manage to take advantage of these tailwinds.

To wrap up, I give this stock a Buy rating, as I believe there is still an upside. I might not be alone, as JP Morgan increased their ownership in the company just a few weeks ago. I recommend keeping a close eye on new equipment sales in the upcoming quarters.
